title: Indonesian Documentary Breaks the Silence on the Plight of Indigenous Papuans
gist: A controversial Indonesian documentary, 'Pig Feast: Colonialism in Our Time,' argues that Jakarta's National Strategic Project (PSN) has furthered oppression of Indigenous Papuans through state-led development, creating what the filmmakers call the world's largest ongoing deforestation project.

insights.claim: Dandhy Laksono and Cypri Dale's 95-minute documentary 'Pig Feast: Colonialism in Our Time,' released in late May 2026, argues the Indonesian state's National Strategic Project (PSN) has furthered historical oppression of Indigenous Papuans.
insights.speaker: Phone Min Thant
insights.so_what: Rising domestic scrutiny of PSN raises ESG and social-license risk for projects and investors in Papua.

insights.claim: The filmmakers describe Indonesia's PSN as having created the world's largest ongoing deforestation project, protected by what they call Jakarta's authoritarian military arm.
insights.speaker: Phone Min Thant
insights.so_what: Large-scale Papuan deforestation tied to state agribusiness/energy projects is a material environmental and reputational flashpoint.

insights.claim: Indonesia's PSN prioritized food and energy security for the majority at the expense of local Papuan communities and ecosystems, per the documentary.
insights.speaker: Phone Min Thant
insights.so_what: Frames the core tension between Jakarta's food/energy-security megaprojects and Indigenous land rights that could fuel unrest.
